ocr.text: In the context of evaluating the efficacy of feature selection techniques in predictive modeling, particularly within the
framework of the C5.0 algorithm, the accompanying data table presents a comparative analysis of the model's performance
under varying conditions. The table delineates scenarios with and without feature selection, allowing for a nuanced
understanding of how these techniques influence key metrics such as Absorbance and Prevention of lysis (%). Notably, the C5.0
model achieved an impressive accuracy rate of 99.31% in both conditions, indicating a robust predictive capability.
Different decision tree models  % Correctness  % Wrongness  Performance evaluation (T)  Performance evaluation (F)  The most important feature (protein attribute) in build the decision tree

Without feature selection  With feature selection  Without feature selection  With feature selection  Without feature selection  With feature selection  Without feature selection  With feature selection
QUEST  98.97  99.31  1.03  0.69  -  0.053  -  2.833  Frequency of Leu - Leu
CHAID  99.66  99.31  0.34  0.69  -  0.053  -  2.833  Frequency of oxygen
Exhaustive CHAID  99.66  99.31  0.34  0.69  -  0.053  -  2.833  Frequency of oxygen
Concentration (μg/mL)  Absorbance  Prevention of lysis (%)
Aspirin  0.148  38.84
Negative control  0.242  0
However, examining the Absorbance values reveals a significant differentiation in the data, with the feature selection approach
yielding a lower Absorbance reading of 0.162 at a concentration of 300 μg/mL, as opposed to higher values recorded without
feature selection. This discrepancy underscores the potential impact of feature selection on model interpretability and outcome
reliability. Furthermore, the Prevention of lysis, which is critical for assessing the overall effectiveness of the treatment, also
merits attention as it provides insight into the biological relevance of the results. The implications of these findings highlight the
importance of feature selection in enhancing model performance and data clarity within the scope of biochemical analysis.
